perturbation.original_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by your or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity provided however any suit for damages shall be specifically limited to actual provable damages, and Company hereby waives any and all rights to seek consequential, punitive, treble or other compensatory damages of any types or kind.
perturbation.changed_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by your or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity provided however any suit for damages shall be specifically limited to actual provable damages, and Company hereby waives any and all rights to seek consequential damages.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'punitive, treble or other compensatory damages of any types or kind,' the company waives rights to remedies that might be available to them under California law. This introduces legal risk by limiting the company's potential recovery in case of breach, potentially violating the principle of making the injured party whole.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3294 allows for punitive damages in cases of oppression, fraud, or malice. The modified text explicitly waives the company's right to seek punitive damages, even if such grounds exist, contradicting the potential remedies available under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Damages in General [3281 - 3296] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Exemplary Damages [3294 - 3296] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 3294. (a) In an action for the breach of an obligation not arising from contract, where it is proven by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice, the plaintiff, in addition to the actual damages, may recover damages for the sake of example and by way of punishing the defendant. (b) An employer shall not be liable for damages pursuant to subdivision (a), based upon acts of an employee of the employer, unless the employer had advance knowledge of the unfitness of the employee and employed him or her with a conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others or authorized or ratified the wrongful conduct for which the damages are awarded or was personally guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. With respect to a corporate employer, the advance knowledge and conscious disregard, authorization, ratification or act of oppressi
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the waiver of 'punitive, treble or other compensatory damages of any types or kind,' only waiving 'consequential damages'. According to the law explanation and California Civil Code § 3294, punitive damages are available in cases of oppression, fraud, or malice. By removing the explicit waiver of punitive damages, the modified text contradicts the original agreement where the company had waived rights to these damages, and now, these damages could potentially be sought under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code CIV 3294, states that in cases of oppression, fraud, or malice, 'the plaintiff, in addition to the actual damages, may recover damages for the sake of example and by way of punishing the defendant.' This supports the claim that the original text was modified in a way that introduces a legal risk by limiting the company's potential waiver of remedies.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware could potentially remove certain consumer protections afforded under California law if the subject matter involves transactions or activities that primarily occur in California. Delaware law may offer less protection to consumers in certain scenarios, thus omitting legally mandated consumer rights under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA, Cal. Civ. Code § 1750 et seq.) provides specific remedies for consumers against unfair or deceptive business practices. By switching the governing law to Delaware, the contract attempts to circumvent these California-specific consumer protections that would otherwise apply if the transaction or activity occurs primarily within California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1750 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Delaware, potentially circumventing consumer protections provided by California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA), as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation states this could result in omitting legally mandated consumer rights under California law if the transaction primarily occurs there. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA, Cal. Civ. Code § 1750 et seq.) suggesting the change introduces a contradiction because Delaware law might not offer the same level of protection, thus removing legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Although the Company has endeavored to include in the Evaluation Materials information which it believes to be relevant for the purpose of your investigation, you acknowledge and agree that none of the Company or the Company's representatives or agents is making any representation or warranty, expressed or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Materials, and none of the Company or the Company's other representatives or agents, nor any of their respective officers, directors, employees, representatives, stockholders, owners, affiliates, advisors or agents, will have any liability to you or any other person resulting from the use of Evaluation Materials by you or any of your representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Although the Company has endeavored to include in the Evaluation Materials information which it believes to be relevant for the purpose of your investigation, you acknowledge and agree that none of the Company or the Company's representatives or agents is making any representation or warranty, expressed or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Materials.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the liability clause ('and none of the Company or the Company's other representatives or agents, nor any of their respective officers, directors, employees, representatives, stockholders, owners, affiliates, advisors or agents, will have any liability to you or any other person resulting from the use of Evaluation Materials by you or any of your representatives.') could be problematic under California law related to negligent misrepresentation. California law allows for claims based on negligent misrepresentation, and this clause attempts to broadly waive all liability, which might not be enforceable, but the omission changes the dynamic.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1710 defines deceit, which can include the assertion of something as a fact that is not true by one who has no reasonable ground for believing it to be true (negligent misrepresentation). While the original text attempts to waive all liability, its removal introduces legal risk because a waiver of liability for negligent misrepresentation might not be fully enforceable under California law, potentially creating a loophole. The changed text makes the issue more ambiguous.
perturbation.location: Page 4, No Representation or Warranty Section
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1710. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1710. A deceit, within the meaning of the last section, is either: 1. The suggestion, as a fact, of that which is not true, by one who does not believe it to be true; 2. The assertion, as a fact, of that which is not true, by one who has no reasonable ground for believing it to be true; 3. The suppression of a fact, by one who is bound to disclose it, or who gives information of other facts which are likely to mislead for want of communication of that fact; or, 4. A promise, made without any intention of performing it. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of the liability clause directly impacts potential legal recourse for negligent misrepresentation. California Civil Code § 1710 defines deceit, including asserting something as a fact without reasonable grounds for believing it to be true. The original text attempts to waive liability, but its removal, as noted in the Perturbation Explanation, introduces legal risk because such a waiver might not be fully enforceable. The Changed Text exacerbates this risk, increasing exposure to claims of negligent misrepresentation under California law as described in the Law Explanation. The omitted clause, intended to protect the company, leaves them open to legal challenges that the original text sought to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials, and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company,' the contract eliminates a key verification mechanism. This weakens the company's ability to enforce the return or destruction of confidential materials, which could run counter to the duty to protect trade secrets under California law. California law protects trade secrets, and the certification provides evidence of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), codified in Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq., protects trade secrets. While this agreement concerns confidential information rather than necessarily trade secrets, the removal of the certification requirement diminishes the Company's ability to ensure compliance with the requirement to protect the confidentiality of the Evaluation Materials. This may result in a situation where trade secrets could potentially be compromised.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of the certification requirement directly weakens the company's ability to enforce the return or destruction of confidential materials. This is relevant because, as the Law Explanation states, it diminishes the Company's ability to ensure compliance with the requirement to protect the confidentiality of the Evaluation Materials. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this runs counter to the duty to protect trade secrets, as the certification provides evidence of compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that California law, specifically the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, protects trade secrets (although this specific snippet only provides the citation). The removal of the certification step creates a loophole that directly contradicts the aim of safeguarding sensitive information, increasing the risk of potential trade secret misappropriation or unauthorized use.
